meta.title: The Daily Reflection Tree
meta.description: A deterministic end-of-day reflection tool covering three psychological axes: Locus of Control, Contribution Orientation, and Radius of Concern.

nodes.text:
Good evening. You made it to the end of another day.

This isn't a performance review. There's no right answer. It's just ten minutes — you, your day, and a few honest questions.

Ready?
Let's start simply. If today were a passenger in a car — what was your role?

options.label: I was driving — I set the direction and managed what came up
I had a map but someone else was at the wheel
I went where the day took me — I was mostly reacting
I felt stuck — like the car broke down and I couldn't move

nodes.text: You were in the driver's seat today — even if the road was rough. Think about a specific moment when something didn't go as planned. What did you do?

options.label: I adjusted my approach on the fly
I asked for help or input proactively
I absorbed it and kept moving without much reaction
I named the problem clearly so others could act on it

nodes.text: That's an honest answer. Days can be like that. When things weren't going your way, what occupied most of your mental energy?

options.label: Wishing the situation were different
Frustration with what others did or didn't do
Wondering if I'm capable enough for this
Trying to find what, if anything, I could change

nodes.text: Even on the most reactive of days, there's usually one moment where you made a call — maybe a small one. Did you have one today?

options.label: Yes — a small one. I chose how I responded to at least one thing
Actually yes — I made a deliberate choice, I just didn't frame it that way
Honestly, I'm not sure — I was mostly on autopilot
Not really — I felt genuinely without options today

nodes.text:
You said you were "{A1_OPEN.answer}" today — and you proved it. When the road changed, you recalibrated instead of waiting for the road to fix itself.

That's not a small thing. Agency is a practice, and today you practiced it.

There's a version of agency that's quiet — no drama, no announcement. You absorbed the disruption and kept moving. That's a form of resilience that often goes unnoticed, including by yourself.

You were the driver today. Even when the ride was bumpy.

Something important happened in your answer: even on a hard day, part of your mind was looking for what you could change.

That orientation — toward the controllable — is what separates someone who's temporarily stuck from someone who stays stuck. You're not stuck.

You said "{A1_Q_CHOICE.answer}" — and that matters more than you might think.

Most people on a reactive day don't find that moment. You did. Agency isn't about controlling everything — it's about recognizing the choices that are actually yours. You found one today.

Sometimes the most honest thing you can do is admit: today was genuinely hard, and the choices weren't obvious.

That honesty is itself a form of awareness — and awareness is where agency begins. You don't have to pretend today was more empowering than it was. Tomorrow has different ingredients.

You've looked at how you moved through today — the agency you had, or didn't have. Now let's look at what you did with that movement.

Axis 2: Not how you handled things — but what you gave.
Think about your energy today — the mental and emotional bandwidth you brought to work. Where did most of it go?

options.label: Into my own tasks and deliverables
Into helping someone else with theirs
Into trying to make sure my contributions were noticed
Into making sure the team's outcome was good, regardless of credit

nodes.text: You invested in something beyond yourself today. Was there a specific moment when you gave something — time, knowledge, attention — that nobody asked for?

options.label: Yes — I helped someone proactively, no one asked me to
A small thing — a kind word, a minute of listening
I'm not sure it counts — it was just part of my job
Not really — I focused on what was asked of me

nodes.text: Staying in your own lane can be focus — or it can be something else. When you think about your team today, what's the dominant feeling?

options.label: Satisfied — I did what I was supposed to do well
Undervalued — my effort wasn't recognized the way it should've been
A bit disconnected — I was in my own world today
Frustrated — others weren't putting in what I was

nodes.text: "Just part of my job" is an interesting phrase. What if it did count? Which of these happened today — even once?

options.label: I gave someone my real attention when they needed it
I caught something before it became someone else's problem
I acknowledged someone's effort when I didn't have to
Honestly, none of these happened today

nodes.text:
You gave something today that wasn't required. That's the definition of discretionary effort — and it's also the thing that compounds over time in a team.

The people who received it may not have said much. They rarely do. But it registered.

There's value in doing your own work well. But here's a gentle question to carry: when did you last contribute to something that didn't show up in your own metrics?

Not today's question to answer — just one to notice.

Feeling undervalued is real — and it's worth examining. But here's a pattern worth knowing: when we're focused on what we're owed, it becomes very hard to see what we're giving.

Neither feeling makes you a bad person. They just pull attention in opposite directions. Which direction did today need from you?

That's contribution — even if it doesn't feel like it from the inside.

Organizational citizenship research calls this "discretionary behavior" — the invisible glue that keeps teams functioning. It doesn't get logged. It doesn't usually get praised. But you did it.

That's an honest answer. Some days the tank is empty and survival mode is the mode.

The question isn't whether today was a giving day. It's whether you notice the pattern — and whether tomorrow offers a different opening.

You've looked at how you moved, and what you gave. One more lens.

Axis 3: Not just what you did — but who was in your field of view while you did it.
When you picture today's biggest challenge — the thing that took the most out of you — who else is in the frame?

options.label: Just me — it was mine to carry
My team — we were all navigating it
A colleague who had it harder than I did
The person our work is ultimately for — customer, user, stakeholder

nodes.text: Carrying something alone takes real energy. While you were inside that challenge — was there anyone nearby who might have been watching, affected, or depending on how you handled it?

options.label: Yes — I was aware of them, though I was focused on my part
Not at the time, but now that you mention it — yes
Maybe — I'm not sure my experience touched anyone else's
No — this was genuinely isolated to me

nodes.text: You kept the team in your view today — that matters. When you think about a teammate specifically, did you notice how they were doing beyond the task?

options.label: Yes — I actively checked in or noticed something beyond the work
Somewhat — I was aware but stayed in my own lane
Mostly task-level — I tracked their work, not their experience
No — I didn't have the bandwidth today

nodes.text: You held someone else in your awareness today — that's a wide field of view. Did that awareness change how you acted?

options.label: Yes — I did something differently because of it
It shifted my perspective, even if not my actions
I'm not sure — it was more background awareness than active thought
No — I noticed but it didn't change what I did

nodes.text:
You oriented toward others today — not abstractly, but concretely. Maslow called this self-transcendence: not the denial of your own needs, but the expansion of what you're working for.

The research on meaning at work points here consistently: the days that feel most significant are almost never about what we received.

Here's something worth sitting with: you were carrying something that felt personal — and you could still hold others in your awareness.

That's not trivial. Stress narrows our field of view. The fact that yours stayed wide, even a little, says something.

You kept the team in your framing — that's the starting point. But there's a level beyond tracking what others are doing: noticing how they're experiencing it.

Perspective-taking — really imagining another person's inner state — changes the quality of your presence, not just your awareness.

Some days the challenge is so consuming that everything else contracts to a point.

There's no shame in it. But here's an experiment for tomorrow: before you start working, name one person whose day you'll pay attention to — not to fix anything, just to notice. It costs almost nothing and tends to shift the entire texture of the day.

Awareness without action is still awareness — and awareness is where change begins.

You held someone else in your field today. Whether or not it changed your behavior, it changed your framing. Batson's research on perspective-taking suggests this matters: even the act of wondering about another person's experience creates a different quality of presence.

Here's what today looked like from the inside:

**Locus:** You leaned {axis1.dominant} today — {axis1.summary_text}.

**Orientation:** Your energy moved {axis2.dominant} — {axis2.summary_text}.

**Radius:** Your field of view was {axis3.dominant} — {axis3.summary_text}.

---

{combined_reflection}

You answered honestly. That's the whole point.

axis1.internal: you stayed in the driver's seat — or found your way back to it
axis1.external: circumstances had more pull today — and you're aware of it

axis2.contribution: toward others — giving more than was required
axis2.entitlement: inward — tracking what you received more than what you gave

axis3.other: wide — others were in the picture
axis3.self: narrow — today was a single-person frame

combined.internal_contribution_other: Three axes, one pattern: you showed up for something beyond yourself today. That's not an accident — it's a practice.
combined.internal_contribution_self: You moved well and gave generously — but the lens stayed mostly on you. Tomorrow might be the day to widen it.
combined.internal_entitlement_other: Your agency was real, and so was your awareness of others. The middle axis — contribution — is the bridge between the two.
combined.internal_entitlement_self: You had agency today — that's real. The next layer is directing that agency outward. What would it look like to be as effective for someone else as you were for yourself?
combined.external_contribution_other: Even when the day felt out of your control, you gave — and you noticed others. That combination is quietly powerful.
combined.external_contribution_self: You gave to others even on a hard day. The framing stayed close — but the generosity was there.
combined.external_entitlement_other: A reactive day, and the pull was inward — and yet you kept others in your awareness. That's more than most people do on a hard day.
combined.external_entitlement_self: Today was hard, and the frame stayed tight around your own experience. That's understandable. Tomorrow is a new set of variables.

nodes.text:
See you tomorrow evening.

Bring whatever the day gives you.
